citation_contexts: A combination of livestock production, industrial farming techniques, and human exposure to zoonotic pathogens makes biosecurity measures an increasing necessity (Bhatia et al. 2024).

Build Non-invasive Bio-banks: Systematically archive fecal, saliva, and environmental DNA (eDNA) samples from bats, rodents, and wild non-human primates to uncover hidden viral diversity without harming native ecosystems [196].

Continued innovation in these areas will facilitate the discovery of novel viruses and the elucidation of complex virus-host interactions, which are critical for predicting zoonotic spillover events [17].

This close proximity facilitates the interspecies transmission of an estimated 75% of all emerging virus-associated infectious diseases , as demonstrated by recent outbreaks like Zika, Ebola, and COVID-19 [17].

Wastewater surveillance: capabilities and limitations : The application of viral metagenomics to waste-water surveillance provides a centralized, cost-effective method for monitoring viral spread within communities, as demonstrated during the SARS-CoV-2 pandem...[truncated]

In this context, zoological gardens, poultry, and cattle farms are high-risk zones for pathogen spillover, as they are situated in high-density human areas and facilitate animal-public interaction on a daily basis (Bhatia et al., 2024; Kuroda et al., 2024).

…context, cattle, poultry, sheep, and goat farms provide ideal breeding grounds in semiurban or rural settings for mosquitoes, supporting the circulation of arboviruses and bacterial pathogens, increasing the risk of spillover to nearby human populations (Bhat...[truncated]

Livestock farms and zoological enclosures are well-recognized interfaces for potential zoonotic spillovers, given the close proximity of humans, domestic animals, and wildlife (Bhatia et al., 2024; Kuroda et al., 2024).

Fruit bats from the Pteropodidae family are thought to be its natural reservoir, playing a role in maintaining the virus’s presence in the environment and enabling its spread to other wildlife, such as non-human primates, before ultimately infecting humans (Bh...[truncated]
For example, frequent direct contact between humans and animals increases the probability of pathogens crossing species boundaries, thereby fostering the emergence of novel pathogens [14].
Especially, RNA viruses represent a major threat due to their ability to cross the species barrier and cause new infections in humans [2–5].
